ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to durian pudding; mapped to generic pudding food product class as closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers puddings and similar desserts; no durian-specific pudding code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for durian pudding as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of durian pudding is one individual pudding cup of approximately 100 g, commonly sold as a chilled dessert in Singapore hawker centres and dessert shops.

nutrient_source.title: Durian Pudding — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as sourced directly from HPB Foodo. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 2.8 g/100g based on typical coconut milk pudding composition from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, Pudding, coconut, ready-to-eat, FNDDS) as a secondary cross-reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Durian and Durian-based Desserts — analogous estimate based on durian fruit GI and pudding matrix
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for durian pudding found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 49 (low) is estimated from the GI of durian fruit (GI ~49–55, low-to-medium range per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables) adjusted downward by the pudding matrix effect (gelatin/agar setting, fat from coconut milk, and protein content slow gastric emptying). Glycemic load of 10.7 per 100 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (49 × 21.9) / 100 = 10.7. This is a defensible analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (12.3 g/100g) promoting insulin-driven sodium retention despite low sodium (48 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit durian pudding to occasional small portions (≤50 g) because the 12.3 g/100g added sugar load can promote insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation, offsetting the benefit of its low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (12.3 g/100g) and total carbohydrate (21.9 g/100g) with only moderate fibre (2.8 g/100g) producing a meaningful glycaemic load of 10.7 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of durian pudding; if consumed, restrict to a maximum of 50 g per occasion and pair with a high-protein or high-fibre food to attenuate the postprandial glucose response driven by 12.3 g sugar and 21.9 g total carbohydrate per 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~2.8 g/100g from coconut milk base) and high added sugar (12.3 g/100g) contributing to atherogenic lipid profile risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat durian pudding as an occasional indulgence only, as the combination of approximately 2.8 g saturated fat and 12.3 g added sugar per 100 g may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels with regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Durian pudding (榴莲布丁; Puding durian) is a chilled Southeast Asian dessert made by blending fresh or frozen durian pulp with sugar and setting the mixture with gelatin or agar, often enriched with coconut milk or cream to produce a smooth, creamy texture. It is widely sold in Singapore hawker centres, dessert shops, and supermarkets as individual chilled cups. The flavour is intensely sweet and custardy with the characteristic pungent, sulphurous aroma of durian, balanced by the mild sweetness of coconut milk. The texture is soft, silky, and slightly wobbly — similar to a panna cotta or blancmange. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 139 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 4.4 g fat, 21.9 g carbohydrate, 12.3 g sugar, 2.8 g dietary fibre, and 48 mg sodium, with 50 mg calcium and 0.1 mg iron per HPB Singapore data. The glycaemic index is estimated at 49 (low) with a glycaemic load of 10.7 per 100 g serving. Clinically, the high added sugar content (12.3 g/100g) makes this dessert unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The saturated fat from coconut milk (~2.8 g/100g estimated) adds further cardiovascular caution. Durian pudding is gluten-free and dairy-free but may contain porcine gelatin, making it unsuitable for some religious dietary requirements. It is best enjoyed as an occasional treat in small portions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in durian pudding?
Is durian pudding high in sugar?
What is the GI of durian pudding?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 for all fields. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue (coconut milk pudding). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database durian fruit data with pudding matrix adjustment, documented transparently. All mandatory Singaporean multilingual names are present.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for durian pudding and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for a directly equivalent product as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g of 2.8 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest analogue (coconut milk-based pudding) and is not directly reported by HPB for this entry. GI of 49 is an analogous estimate based on durian fruit GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct measurement for durian pudding is not available in any approved source. Halal status is marked false due to potential porcine gelatin; products using agar-agar or halal-certified gelatin would qualify as halal — consumers should verify product labelling. No zero-value core macros are present in this record; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
